summary: I work as a clinical nurse educator at the Duke University School of Nursing, where I facilitate learning experiences for ABSN and master's students in didactic, clinical, and lab settings. Recently, I've been teaching in the lab and clinical environments, helping students care for patients. The School of Nursing has been a central place for me to support future nurses during the pandemic.

The COVID-19 pandemic has deeply affected my life and work since early 2020. I remember traveling to New York City in February 2020 before the pandemic was declared, and then watching the daily updates on CNN with my son as the virus spread. My family experienced COVID firsthand when two of my sons tested positive and recovered. The pandemic also changed how I interact with colleagues and students, limiting physical contact and creating a lot of fear and uncertainty. Despite initial skepticism, I chose to get vaccinated to protect myself and others.

I used to work in the pharmaceutical industry, which gave me insight into how things sometimes lack transparency and are driven by monetary gain. This background made me initially skeptical about the rapid development of the COVID-19 vaccine. I thought deeply about the clinical trials, especially regarding people of color, before deciding to trust the science and get vaccinated. My experience in pharma informed my cautious but ultimately supportive stance on vaccination.

One of my sons traveled to Pennsylvania, specifically Philadelphia, where he contracted COVID-19. Thankfully, he recovered well from the virus. This experience made us realize the importance of vaccination and taking precautions seriously.

Philadelphia is where my son was when he contracted COVID-19 during his travels. It was a worrying time for our family, but he recovered without complications. This event underscored the reality of the pandemic and the need for vigilance.

In February 2020, before the pandemic was fully recognized, my daughters and I traveled to New York City to tour New York University, my daughter's dream school. We returned home unaware of how serious the virus would become globally. Later, my son and I would watch CNN daily to track the pandemic's progression from that point onward.

My daughter dreamed of attending New York University, so we traveled there in February 2020 for a campus tour. At that time, the COVID-19 virus was just beginning to emerge globally, and we had no idea it would soon change our lives. That visit was one of the last normal trips we took before the pandemic fully hit.

During the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, my son and I would watch the ticker tape on CNN every day to keep up with the latest case numbers and vaccine developments. We became amateur epidemiologists, trying to estimate the impact and timeline of the virus. CNN was a key source of information for us during that uncertain time.

My oldest son lives in Florida, and he was one of the family members who contracted COVID-19 early in the pandemic. He experienced symptoms but recovered well, likely due to his good health and fitness. His illness made us more vigilant about safety and vaccination.

My husband worked in the intensive care unit during the height of the pandemic, caring for very sick COVID-19 patients who required proning and ECMO. His frontline experience gave me a close-up view of the severity of the virus and the challenges healthcare workers faced. This also influenced our family's decisions around safety and vaccination.

As a Black woman with five Black sons, the murder of George Floyd deeply affected me during the pandemic. It was a time of dual pandemics—COVID-19 and racial violence—that created immense emotional pressure. The day of Derek Chauvin's trial, when we spoke, was especially heavy, highlighting the ongoing challenges we face.

We spoke on the day of Derek Chauvin's trial, which was a very difficult and emotional day for me. The trial was connected to the murder of George Floyd, an event that deeply affected me as a Black woman. This moment underscored the racial tensions occurring simultaneously with the COVID-19 pandemic.

entity: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
summary: The CDC tried to provide leadership and guidance during the pandemic, but I felt there were missed opportunities for clearer directives and better communication. The evolving information and sometimes unclear guidance contributed to fear and confusion among healthcare workers and the public. Despite this, I relied on scientific data to make informed decisions about vaccination and safety.
